title: Recommender Systems with Generative Retrieval (TIGER)
authors: Shashank Rajput, Nikhil Mehta, Anima Singh, Raghunandan H. Keshavan, Trung Vu, Lukasz Heldt, Lichan Hong, Yi Tay, Vinh Q. Tran, Jonah Samost, Maciej Kula, Ed H. Chi, Maheswaran Sathiamoorthy (Google DeepMind / Google Research)
tokenization_method: Semantic IDs via RQ-VAE (Residual-Quantized Variational Autoencoder). Each item is first turned into a single 768-d content embedding by a frozen Sentence-T5 over a text concatenation of the item's metadata (title, price, brand, category). An RQ-VAE encoder (MLP 768->512->256->128->32) compresses this to a 32-d latent z. Residual quantization then recursively encodes z with m=3 separate codebooks (one per level), each of cardinality 256 and dimension 32: at level d the current residual r_d is snapped to its nearest codebook vector e_{c_d}, the next residual is r_{d+1}=r_d-e_{c_d}, and the chosen indices (c_0,c_1,c_2) form a coarse-to-fine 3-tuple of discrete codewords = the item's Semantic ID. A 4th disambiguation token is appended when two distinct items collide to the same 3-tuple, giving a final length-4 ID. So a complex item is tokenized as a short ordered tuple of learned discrete codes rather than one atomic ID or a dense vector.
feature_types_handled: In TIGER's native domain: a single fused ITEM-level content embedding derived from mixed item metadata (text title/brand/category + numeric price) that Sentence-T5 has already collapsed into one 768-d vector. The RQ-VAE itself sees only that one continuous vector per item — it does NOT operate per-feature, per-column, or on raw heterogeneous fields. There is no native numerical/categorical/temporal/missing typing inside the quantizer; all heterogeneity is pushed upstream into the text-serialization + Sentence-T5 step.
token_granularity: learned code, hierarchical. One item -> a tuple of m=3 (+1 collision) discrete codewords drawn from per-level codebooks. Each codeword is a learned-code token; the tuple is coarse-to-fine (level 0 = coarsest semantic cluster, level 2 = finest). This is the 'learned code' granularity option in the topic's token-granularity layer, and crucially a MULTI-token-per-entity scheme (unlike one-token-per-feature in FT-Transformer).
numerical_strategy: No dedicated numerical embedding. Price (the one numeric field) is serialized into the metadata text and absorbed by Sentence-T5; the RQ-VAE only ever quantizes the resulting dense embedding. There is no piecewise-linear, periodic, or quantile numeric encoding here — the discretization is of a holistic embedding, not of individual continuous features.
categorical_strategy: No per-field categorical embedding table. Brand/category strings are concatenated into the item text and encoded by Sentence-T5; the RQ-VAE codebook then assigns shared, learned discrete codes. This is the conceptual opposite of entity-embeddings: instead of one lookup table per field, items share a small set of hierarchical codebooks whose codes capture semantic clusters spanning all fields jointly.
high_cardinality_handling: This is the strongest transfer hook. A corpus of N items (potentially millions of distinct 'IDs', i.e. extreme cardinality) is represented by only m codebooks x 256 codes = 1024 codeword tokens (3x256 + collision). RQ-VAE thus turns a near-unbounded-cardinality entity space into a fixed, small vocabulary with hierarchical structure, so similar items share prefix codes and rare/new items inherit a sensible code via their content embedding. Codebook collapse (most items snapping to a few codes) is the known risk.
temporal_handling: None inside the tokenizer. Temporality lives only at the sequence level: a user's chronologically ordered item history is flattened into a sequence of semantic-ID tuples fed to the seq2seq model. The RQ-VAE has no time axis; there is no patching, recurrence, or static/time-varying fusion.
scales_to_many_features: Does NOT demonstrate scaling to many features in this topic's sense. TIGER scales in the NUMBER OF ITEMS (corpus cardinality) and in generative-retrieval cost (decoding ~4 tokens beam-search instead of dot-product over N item embeddings), not in the number of heterogeneous input FEATURES. The quantizer's input is always one fixed 768-d vector regardless of how many raw fields fed Sentence-T5. So 'scaling' evidence here is orthogonal to the 70+-feature axis; the per-feature scaling question is simply not tested.
downstream_model: A small encoder-decoder Transformer (T5-style): 4 encoder + 4 decoder layers, 6 heads of dim 64, d_model 128, MLP dim 1024, dropout 0.1, ~13M params. Vocabulary = 1024 semantic codeword tokens (256x4 levels) + 2000 hashed user-ID tokens. It consumes a user's history as a flat token stream (user token, then each item's m-tuple concatenated) and autoregressively GENERATES the next item's semantic ID tuple token-by-token (beam search), turning retrieval into sequence generation.
pretraining_objective: Two-stage, both task/self-supervised. Stage 1: RQ-VAE trained with reconstruction loss ||x - x_hat||^2 + RQ loss sum_i(||sg[r_i]-e_{c_i}||^2 + beta||r_i-sg[e_{c_i}]||^2), beta=0.25, sg=stop-gradient (standard VQ-VAE-style codebook+commitment loss), 20k epochs, Adagrad lr=0.4. Stage 2: the seq2seq model trained with next-item (next-semantic-ID) prediction, i.e. autoregressive language-model-style objective over codeword tokens. There is NO target/label-aware supervision of the codes; the Semantic IDs are unsupervised w.r.t. the recommendation target.
key_results: Amazon Beauty / Sports & Outdoors / Toys & Games. TIGER beats the best baseline on every dataset/metric. Beauty: Recall@5 0.0454 vs S3-Rec 0.0387 (+17.3%), NDCG@5 0.0321 vs SASRec 0.0249 (+29.0%). Sports: Recall@5 0.0264 vs S3-Rec 0.0251 (+5.2%), NDCG@5 0.0181 vs S3-Rec 0.0161 (+12.6%). Toys: Recall@5 0.0521 vs SASRec 0.0463 (+12.5%), NDCG@5 0.0371 vs SASRec 0.0306 (+21.2%). Invalid generated IDs (no matching item) are rare: ~0.1-1.6% at top-10. Cold-start: on 5% held-out unseen items (Beauty), TIGER beats a Semantic-KNN baseline across K. Temperature sampling tunes recommendation diversity (Entropy@10: 0.76 at T=1.0 -> 1.38 at T=2.0).
baselines_compared: Sequential recommenders: SASRec, S3-Rec, GRU4Rec, BERT4Rec, FDSA, HGN, P5, plus an ID-based generative-retrieval ablation. For the tokenizer ablation: RQ-VAE Semantic IDs vs LSH-based Semantic IDs vs Random/independent IDs. Cold-start vs Semantic-KNN.
transferable_idea: Use RQ-VAE residual quantization to turn an arbitrarily-high-cardinality entity (here an item; for this topic, a high-cardinality categorical value, an entire feature-row, or a temporal patch) into a SHORT ORDERED TUPLE of hierarchical discrete codes from a small shared codebook (e.g. 3 levels x 256), where coarse-to-fine codes make similar entities share prefixes and cold/rare entities inherit a code from their content embedding instead of an OOV bucket. Concretely for 70+ heterogeneous features: (a) replace per-field high-cardinality embedding tables with shared RQ-VAE codes to bound vocabulary and give graceful OOV; (b) optionally quantize a whole feature-row embedding into a few semantic-ID tokens to compress a 70-feature row into a short transformer sequence (granularity = learned code), cutting attention length.
limitations: Authors-stated/observed: (1) Generated IDs can be invalid (must filter / constrain decoding). (2) Codebook collapse / uneven code usage is a known RQ-VAE failure mode requiring care (k-means init, etc.). (3) Demonstrated only at modest scale (10-35k users, 12-18k items) on Amazon review subsets; industrial-scale behavior is later work (RPG, LIGER, EAGER). (4) Two-stage training: codes are frozen before the seq2seq stage, so they cannot adapt to the downstream loss. (5) The whole approach is built for next-item generative RETRIEVAL, not for tabular prediction.
pitfalls: 1) SCOPE TRAP (the topic's explicit anti-pattern): TIGER is a recsys-native semantic-ID system; importing it wholesale is out of scope. ONLY the RQ-VAE discretize-an-embedding-into-hierarchical-codes idea transfers. The seq2seq generative-retrieval machinery, the user-history flattening, and the next-item objective are recsys-specific and irrelevant to a 70+-feature tabular tokenizer. 2) The quantizer never sees raw heterogeneous features — it sees ONE pre-fused 768-d embedding. So 'it handles complex items' is really 'Sentence-T5 + text serialization handles them, then we compress one vector.' To use the idea for tabular features you must FIRST decide what embedding to quantize (per-row? per-field-group? per-temporal-patch?) — TIGER gives no guidance, this is the real design work. 3) Reported gains (+5-29%) are vs sequential-recsys baselines on Amazon data; they say NOTHING about tabular-prediction accuracy and must not be cited as evidence the tokenizer helps tabular models. 4) RQ-VAE adds a separate offline training stage + collapse risk + non-adaptive frozen codes — a real engineering cost versus simply learning embeddings end-to-end. 5) Two items can collide to the same tuple (needs a disambiguation token), so codes are NOT guaranteed unique/lossless.
relevance_to_topic: High-value but narrow: it is the canonical demonstration that RQ-VAE residual quantization can convert an extreme-cardinality entity into a short hierarchical discrete-code tuple with graceful cold-start — directly relevant to the discretization-vq route and to bounding high-cardinality vocabulary for 70+ mixed features — but the rest of the system (generative retrieval, seq2seq next-item) is out of scope and must be stripped away.
code_available: no official Google release; widely reproduced. Community implementations exist (e.g. EdoardoBotta/RQ-VAE-Recommender on GitHub) and the RQ-VAE component is reusable. The transferable RQ-VAE part is small and standard enough to re-implement from VQ-VAE/RQ-VAE primitives.
